title: Vision Transformer (ViT)
summary: Apply standard transformer encoder directly to images by splitting into patches (tokens). Less inductive bias than CNNs (no locality, no translation equivariance built in) → needs more data to match, but scales better with compute.
key_insights: Image → split into $P \times P$ patches → flatten each to vector → linear projection → sequence of tokens
Standard transformer encoder on patch tokens. No convolutions at all in the original design.
Less bias + more data beats more bias + less data. ViT needs ~300M images (JFT) to match ResNet. With enough data, it surpasses.
Patch size = tradeoff. Small patches = long sequence ($O(n^2)$ attention cost). Large patches = lose fine detail.
CLS token: prepend a learnable classification token. Its output representation is used for classification.
Position embeddings are LEARNED (not sinusoidal). 2D structure must be learned from data, not hard-coded.

mathematical_core.patchify: Image $\in \mathbb{R}^{H \times W \times C}$ → $N = HW/P^2$ patches, each $\in \mathbb{R}^{P^2 C}$
mathematical_core.projection: $$z_0 = [x_{\text{cls}}; \; x_1 E; \; x_2 E; \ldots; x_N E] + E_{\text{pos}}$$ $E \in \mathbb{R}^{(P^2 C) \times d}$, $E_{\text{pos}} \in \mathbb{R}^{(N+1) \times d}$
mathematical_core.sequence_length: $N = (224/16)^2 = 196$ patches for standard ViT-Base (224×224 image, 16×16 patches)
mathematical_core.encoder: Standard transformer encoder (LN → Multi-Head Attn → residual → LN → FFN → residual) × $L$ layers

derivations.title: CNN inductive bias vs ViT — what's gained and lost

steps.name: CNN biases
steps.math: Locality: each neuron sees only $k \times k$ neighborhood. Translation equivariance: same kernel everywhere. Hierarchy: built by stacking.

steps.name: ViT has none of these
steps.math: Every patch attends to every other patch from layer 1. No locality constraint. Position must be learned. Hierarchy must emerge from training, not architecture.

steps.name: Scaling behavior
steps.math: CNNs plateau — adding more data or compute gives diminishing returns (bias limits the function family). ViT keeps improving — less bias means the function family is larger.

derivations.consequence: Inductive bias = sample efficiency. No bias = compute efficiency at scale. The crossover point: ~100M+ images. Below that, CNNs win.

derivations.title: Compute cost comparison

steps.name: ViT attention
steps.math: $O(N^2 d) = O((HW/P^2)^2 d)$. For 224×224, P=16: $O(196^2 \cdot 768) \approx 30M$ per head per layer.

steps.name: ResNet conv
steps.math: $O(k^2 C_{\text{in}} C_{\text{out}} H W)$. Early layers: $O(9 \cdot 64 \cdot 64 \cdot 56 \cdot 56) \approx 116M$.

steps.name: Key difference
steps.math: ViT cost is quadratic in image resolution ($H^2 W^2 / P^4$). CNN cost is linear ($HW$). High-res images: CNN is much cheaper.

derivations.consequence: ViT is expensive for high-resolution images. Hybrid approaches (CNN stem + transformer) give best of both worlds.

geometric_properties: Each patch token starts with only local information. Self-attention progressively creates global features.
Attention maps in early layers often resemble convolution kernels (locality emerges from data).
CLS token attention pattern: learns to aggregate from informative patches.
statistical_properties: Pre-training dataset size matters enormously: ViT underperforms ResNet on ImageNet-1K alone.
With JFT-300M pre-training: ViT-H achieves 88.6% ImageNet top-1 (vs ResNet-152: 78.3%).
numerical_properties: Patch size $P = 16$ is the standard. $P = 14$ or $P = 8$ give better accuracy but $4\times$ or $16\times$ longer sequences.
Memory: attention matrix is $[B, h, N, N]$. $N = 196$ is manageable. $N = 3136$ ($P = 4$) is not.

related: FOUNDATIONS.INDUCTIVE_BIAS — less bias = needs more data

notes: Natural bridge topic between CNN and Transformer families. Key design question: how much inductive bias to bake in vs learn from data.
